original_abstract: The Brønsted acidity of proton-exchanged zeolites remains the cornerstone of their industrial success, driving pivotal transformations of hydrocarbons and oxygenates in heterogeneous catalysis. Over several decades, extensive research aimed at unraveling these catalytic mechanisms at the atomic scale has fundamentally reshaped our understanding of how acidity and spatial confinement synergistically dictate reactivity. These evolving concepts now represent a crucial intersection between heterogeneous catalysis and molecular chemistry. This review provides a comprehensive molecular perspective on the mechanisms governing generic transformations at zeolitic Brønsted acid sites, integrating insights from advanced kinetic analysis, in situ and operando spectroscopy, and quantum chemical modeling. We first evaluate the current understanding of the nature of Brønsted acid sites and the governing parameters of zeolitic activity, followed by an in-depth focus on the reactivity of alkenes, alkanes, aromatics, alcohols, and polyhydroxy compounds. Central to these discussions are the elementary steps of C–C, C–H, and C–O bond activation and formation. Finally, we offer an outlook on overcoming future challenges in the field, with the ultimate ambition of establishing a rational framework for the design of next-generation Brønsted acid catalysts.
humanized_abstract: In addition to their traditional use in petrochemistry, proton-exchanged zeolites are currently used in the petroleum industry as catalysts and tumbling catalysts for the conversion of hydrocarbons and oxygenates. Understanding the mechanisms of catalytic conversions on zeolites has been at the center of research activities for decades, and, with increasingly accurate kinetic studies, in situ and operando spectroscopy, the knowledge of how to induce a certain reaction on zeolites has evolved towards a union of the effect of acidity and the role of steric constraints, two concepts that have today become essential in the fields of heterogeneous catalysis and molecular chemistry. This review provides a molecular description of the activity of zeolites based on the fundamental concepts which are derived from catalytic kinetic studies as well as from in situ and operando spectroscopy, completed with quantum chemical calculations. After discussing the description of the Brønsted sites and their catalytic activity, the molecular mechanisms of the different types of catalytic conversion on zeolites (alkene conversions, alkane transformations, aromatic conversions, alcohol transformations and finally polyhydroxy compounds (sugars) are detailed. The elementary steps of the C–C, C–H and C–O bond formations and activations are described, and their corresponding catalytic sites are discussed. A perspective to achieve the future goals of zeolite catalysis is finally discussed, aiming towards the goal of predicting new Brønsted acid catalysts.
